content.text: Prioritization Scoring Framework for Claude Ideas

A prioritization scoring framework gives a program manager an objective, repeatable method for ranking competing Claude AI initiatives. Without one, priority is determined by whoever argues most loudly in the planning meeting. With one, the conversation shifts from 'whose idea wins' to 'what does the data say' — and the scoring model becomes something the whole organization can inspect, challenge, and improve over time.

attrs.alt: Initiative priority matrix plotting business impact against implementation effort, with example initiatives in each quadrant

content.text: The Scoring Dimensions

Six dimensions capture the factors that determine whether a Claude initiative is worth pursuing. Each is scored 1–5 by a human reviewer assisted by Claude's suggestions. The weights reflect the program's priorities and should be reviewed quarterly.
Dimension
Weight
What it measures
Business value
Revenue, cost savings, or strategic value in $ terms
Strategic alignment
Fit with current company or AI strategy OKRs
Implementation feasibility
Technical and compliance complexity (higher = easier)
User reach
Number of users or customers directly affected
Time sensitivity
Penalty for delay — urgency of the need
Reusability
Whether the work creates reusable infrastructure

Business value carries the highest weight because the FinOps program's primary accountability is cost-justified AI investment. Strategic alignment is second because an initiative that doesn't support a current OKR consumes capacity that could serve one that does. Feasibility ranks equally because a high-value initiative blocked by compliance cannot deliver that value.

Weight the dimensions to match your organization's actual priorities, not aspirational ones. If your company is in growth mode, increase user_reach weight. If you're in an efficiency drive, increase business_value. Publish the weights so teams can understand why their initiative scored as it did.
Scoring Rubrics and Model

# scoring_model.py
# Weighted scoring model for Claude initiative prioritization.
# Each dimension is scored 1-5; weights sum to 1.0.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import json

@dataclass
class ScoringDimension:
    name: str
    weight: float
    description: str
    rubric: dict[int, str]   # score -> description of what that score means

DIMENSIONS = [
    ScoringDimension(
        name="business_value",
        weight=0.30,
        description="Revenue generated, cost avoided, or strategic value delivered.",
        rubric={
            1: "< $5K/month impact; vanity metric only",
            2: "$5K–$20K/month or qualitative improvement",
            3: "$20K–$100K/month or clear productivity gain",
            4: "$100K–$500K/month or significant competitive advantage",
            5: "> $500K/month or board-level strategic priority",
        },
    ),
    ScoringDimension(
        name="user_reach",
        weight=0.15,
        description="Number of users or customers directly affected.",
        rubric={
            1: "< 10 internal users",
            2: "10–100 internal users",
            3: "100–1,000 internal users or < 1,000 customers",
            4: "1,000–10,000 customers",
            5: "> 10,000 customers or all enterprise users",
        },
    ),
    ScoringDimension(
        name="strategic_alignment",
        weight=0.20,
        description="Alignment with current company or AI strategy OKRs.",
        rubric={
            1: "No alignment with current priorities",
            2: "Minor alignment; could be deprioritized without impact",
            3: "Moderate alignment; supports a secondary OKR",
            4: "Strong alignment; directly supports a key OKR",
            5: "C-suite priority; explicitly on the roadmap",
        },
    ),
    ScoringDimension(
        name="implementation_feasibility",
        weight=0.20,
        description="Technical and compliance complexity. Higher score = easier.",
        rubric={
            1: "Blocked: compliance issue or missing data; can't proceed",
            2: "High complexity: > 3 months, multiple team dependencies",
            3: "Medium: 1–3 months, one team dependency",
            4: "Low: 2–4 weeks, one team self-contained",
            5: "Trivial: < 2 weeks, single engineer, no new data",
        },
    ),
    ScoringDimension(
        name="time_sensitivity",
        weight=0.10,
        description="Urgency: penalty for delay.",
        rubric={
            1: "No urgency; value is equal 1 year from now",
            2: "Low: marginal benefit to delivering sooner",
            3: "Moderate: competitor pressure or seasonal timing",
            4: "High: regulatory deadline or contract commitment",
            5: "Critical: immediate customer or revenue risk",
        },
    ),
    ScoringDimension(
        name="reusability",
        weight=0.05,
        description="Does this create reusable infrastructure or patterns?",
        rubric={
            1: "One-off; no reuse potential",
            2: "Slightly generalizable with effort",
            3: "Reusable within the team",
            4: "Reusable across 2–5 teams",
            5: "Platform capability: usable org-wide",
        },
    ),
]


@dataclass
class ScoredInitiative:
    title: str
    team: str
    scores: dict[str, int]       # {dimension_name: 1-5}
    notes: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)

    def weighted_score(self, dimensions: list[ScoringDimension] = DIMENSIONS) -> float:
        total = 0.0
        for dim in dimensions:
            score = self.scores.get(dim.name, 0)
            total += score * dim.weight
        return round(total * 2, 2)   # scale to 10

    def quadrant(self) -> str:
        """Classify into priority matrix quadrant."""
        impact_dims = ["business_value", "user_reach", "strategic_alignment"]
        effort_dims = ["implementation_feasibility"]  # invert: high feasibility = low effort
        impact = sum(self.scores.get(d, 0) for d in impact_dims) / len(impact_dims)
        effort_raw = self.scores.get("implementation_feasibility", 3)
        effort = 6 - effort_raw   # invert so 5=easy becomes low_effort=1
        if impact >= 3 and effort <= 3:
            return "quick_win"
        if impact >= 3 and effort > 3:
            return "strategic_bet"
        if impact < 3 and effort <= 3:
            return "fill_in"
        return "time_sink"
Using the rubrics

The rubric for each dimension describes what each score of 1–5 means in concrete terms. Reviewers should read the rubric before scoring — without it, one reviewer's '4' is another's '2'. The rubric also prevents inflation: a score of 5 on business_value requires clear evidence of over $500K/month impact, not a vague statement that the initiative 'could be transformative.'
Claude-Assisted Scoring

Scoring twenty initiatives by hand in a planning meeting is slow and cognitively exhausting. Claude can suggest scores for each dimension based on the intake form fields, leaving the reviewer to accept or override. This cuts scoring time by 50–60% while keeping a human in the decision loop.

# claude_scorer.py
# Use Claude to assist human scorers by providing scoring suggestions
# for each dimension based on the intake form.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SCORING_ASSIST_SYSTEM = """You are a Claude AI program prioritization expert.
Given an initiative description, suggest scores (1-5) for each dimension
and explain the rationale in one sentence per dimension.

Scoring dimensions and their rubrics are provided in the user message.
Output JSON with this structure:
{
  "suggested_scores": {
    "business_value": int,
    "user_reach": int,
    "strategic_alignment": int,
    "implementation_feasibility": int,
    "time_sensitivity": int,
    "reusability": int
  },
  "rationales": {
    "business_value": "string",
    "user_reach": "string",
    "strategic_alignment": "string",
    "implementation_feasibility": "string",
    "time_sensitivity": "string",
    "reusability": "string"
  },
  "concerns": ["any red flags or missing information that affects confidence"]
}
Be conservative — a score of 4 or 5 should require clear evidence in the description."""

def suggest_scores(initiative_description: dict, rubrics: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Given the intake form fields and the scoring rubrics, suggest scores.
    The human reviewer sees these suggestions and can accept or override them.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=800,
        system=SCORING_ASSIST_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Initiative:\n{json.dumps(initiative_description, indent=2)}\n\n"
                f"Scoring rubrics:\n{json.dumps(rubrics, indent=2)}"
            ),
        }],
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)
Show Claude's suggested scores alongside the reviewer's scores in the planning spreadsheet. Track override rate — if reviewers override > 50% of Claude's suggestions, the prompting needs improvement.
Use claude-haiku-4-5 for scoring suggestions. The scoring task is structured and does not require deep reasoning; Haiku produces accurate results at lower cost.
Always show the rationale, not just the score. A suggested score of 3 for feasibility with the rationale 'requires PII access which needs compliance review' gives the reviewer actionable information.
Portfolio Ranking and Priority Tiers

# portfolio_ranker.py
# Rank all pending initiatives and generate a prioritized backlog.

from scoring_model import ScoredInitiative, DIMENSIONS

def rank_initiatives(initiatives: list[ScoredInitiative]) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Rank initiatives by weighted score and enrich with quadrant classification.
    Returns a sorted list of dicts for display in the prioritization meeting.
    """
    ranked = []
    for initiative in initiatives:
        score = initiative.weighted_score()
        ranked.append({
            "title": initiative.title,
            "team": initiative.team,
            "weighted_score": score,
            "quadrant": initiative.quadrant(),
            "scores": initiative.scores,
            "priority": (
                "P0" if score >= 8.0 else
                "P1" if score >= 6.5 else
                "P2" if score >= 5.0 else
                "P3"
            ),
        })
    return sorted(ranked, key=lambda x: -x["weighted_score"])


def print_backlog(ranked: list[dict]) -> None:
    print(f"{'P':>3}  {'Score':>6}  {'Quadrant':<14}  {'Team':<20}  Title")
    print("-" * 80)
    for item in ranked:
        print(
            f"{item['priority']:>3}  {item['weighted_score']:>6.2f}  "
            f"{item['quadrant']:<14}  {item['team']:<20}  {item['title']}"
        )

# Example output:
#  P    Score  Quadrant        Team                 Title
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# P0     8.60  quick_win       Platform             Support ticket classifier
# P0     8.10  quick_win       Platform             Email response drafting
# P1     7.20  strategic_bet   Product              Customer copilot
# P1     6.80  strategic_bet   Product              Documentation search
# P2     5.40  fill_in         Analytics            FAQ generation
# P3     3.20  time_sink       Research             General-purpose AGI
Priority tier
Score range
Disposition
P0
Immediate — schedule in current sprint; CoE assigns engineer
P1
Next sprint — include in upcoming quarterly roadmap
P2
Backlog — revisit when P0/P1 capacity frees up
P3
Decline or defer — notify requester with score breakdown

Priority tiers are not queues — a P0 at 8.2 does not automatically go ahead of a P0 at 9.1 just because it arrived first. Within each tier, the planning meeting makes the final sequencing call based on team bandwidth, dependencies, and quarterly commitments.
Calibrating the Model Over Time

A scoring model that doesn't predict actual outcomes is a bureaucracy tax with no benefit. After completing at least five initiatives, run the calibration check to see whether the model's predictions correlate with delivered value. A correlation above 0.7 means the model is working.

# calibration.py
# Calibrate the scoring model by comparing predicted vs actual value delivery
# for completed initiatives.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class OutcomeRecord:
    title: str
    predicted_score: float
    predicted_value_usd_month: float
    actual_value_usd_month: Optional[float]   # None if not yet measured
    completed: bool
    outcome_notes: str

def calibration_report(outcomes: list[OutcomeRecord]) -> dict:
    """
    Measure how well the scoring model predicts actual value.
    A well-calibrated model should have correlation > 0.7 between
    predicted score and actual value delivered.
    """
    measured = [o for o in outcomes if o.actual_value_usd_month is not None]
    if len(measured) < 5:
        return {"status": "insufficient_data", "n": len(measured)}

    pairs = [(o.predicted_score, o.actual_value_usd_month) for o in measured]
    n = len(pairs)
    mean_x = sum(p[0] for p in pairs) / n
    mean_y = sum(p[1] for p in pairs) / n
    cov = sum((p[0] - mean_x) * (p[1] - mean_y) for p in pairs) / n
    std_x = (sum((p[0] - mean_x) ** 2 for p in pairs) / n) ** 0.5
    std_y = (sum((p[1] - mean_y) ** 2 for p in pairs) / n) ** 0.5
    correlation = cov / (std_x * std_y) if std_x and std_y else 0

    overestimated = [o for o in measured if o.predicted_value_usd_month > o.actual_value_usd_month * 1.3]
    return {
        "n_measured": n,
        "score_to_value_correlation": round(correlation, 3),
        "model_quality": "good" if correlation > 0.7 else "needs_recalibration",
        "overestimated_pct": round(len(overestimated) / n * 100, 1),
        "recommendation": (
            "Model is predictive — continue using current weights."
            if correlation > 0.7
            else "Review dimension weights. Business_value or feasibility may be overweighted."
        ),
    }
Quarterly calibration review:
Run the calibration report at the end of each quarter. Present the results in the monthly FinOps KPI report.
Adjust weights when correlation drops:
If business_value weight is 30% but the dimension has low correlation with actual outcomes, reduce it and increase strategic_alignment.
Track overestimation separately:
Initiatives that were overestimated (predicted > actual by > 30%) reveal systematic biases — often optimistic value claims that should have been probed harder.
Don't adjust the model too frequently:
Changing weights every month makes historical comparisons meaningless. Adjust at most once per quarter, with documented rationale.
Common Scoring Mistakes
Mistake
Symptom
Fix
Halo effect
High-profile initiatives score high on all dimensions
Score each dimension independently; show rubric during scoring
Score inflation
Average score creeps toward 7–8 over time
Require that < 20% of initiatives score P0; enforce rubric strictly
Ignoring feasibility
P0 queue filled with compliance-blocked requests
Make feasibility block: score 1 on feasibility = automatic hold
Single-reviewer bias
Scores reflect the reviewer's team preferences
Require two independent scorers; average when they differ by > 2
Stale scores
Scores from 6 months ago drive current decisions
Re-score any initiative that has been in backlog > 90 days
Summary

A prioritization scoring framework for Claude initiatives uses six weighted dimensions — business value, strategic alignment, feasibility, user reach, time sensitivity, and reusability — to produce a composite score that routes initiatives to P0–P3 priority tiers. Claude assists by suggesting scores for each dimension from the intake form, cutting scoring time by half while keeping human judgment in the loop. The portfolio ranker produces a sorted backlog that the planning meeting uses for sequencing rather than starting from scratch each sprint. Quarterly calibration checks whether the model's predictions match actual delivered value, and dimension weights are adjusted when they don't. A well-calibrated model makes the prioritization conversation data-driven and auditable rather than political.
